prompt: Which work in the corpus maps the human body onto the seven mountains, seven oceans, and seven continents with specific organ-by-organ identifications — equating the kidneys with Mount Mālyavān, the heart with Mount Mahāmeru, the liver with Mount Vindhya, the spleen with Mount Gandhamādana; and equating urine with the fresh-water ocean, blood with the ocean of sugarcane juice, sweat with the salt ocean, fat with the oil ocean, saliva with the honey ocean, marrow with the milk ocean, and the brain with the ocean of curds; while bone is Jambudvīpa, sinew is Kuśadvīpa, flesh is Śaṅkhadvīpa, skin is Śālmalidvīpa, and teeth are Krauñcadvīpa? Quote the equations of the seven oceans and seven continents verbatim and identify the work and chapter.
rationale: This organ-by-organ correspondence with the saptaparvata, saptārṇava, and saptadvīpa — including the highly specific 'brain = ocean of curds,' 'blood = ocean of sugarcane juice,' and 'sinew = Kuśadvīpa' identifications — is unique to this Old Javanese Saiva-tattva text; Vrhaspatitattva and Gaṇapatitattva enumerate the same Saptadvīpa and tattva systems but never make these body-microcosm equations, and no Sanskrit smṛti or other corpus work pairs Mount Mālyavān with the kidneys or the brain with curds.

prompt: Which work in the corpus describes a graded fourteen-step cosmic devolution in which a being, 'lacking in yoga,' falls down a fixed ladder of births — Tripuruṣa becoming Pañcaṛṣi, Pañcaṛṣi becoming Saptaṛṣi, Saptaṛṣi becoming Devaṛṣi, then Devatā, Vidyādhara, Gandharva, Dānava, Daitya, Rākṣasa, Bhūta Yakṣa, Bhūta Dĕṅĕn, Bhūta Kāla, Bhūta Piśāca, finally human, and at last a being of the triyak realm — and immediately afterwards classifies the five kinds of triyak as pāśu (village domestic animal), mṛga (forest wild beast), pakṣī (bird), mīna (fish in water), and pipīlikā (the belly-mover whose 'other names are not known')? Quote the descent ladder verbatim and identify the work and chapter.
rationale: This precise stepwise descent through fourteen named ranks of beings, each transition explicitly captioned 'if lacking in yoga,' culminating in the five-fold triyak classification with pipīlikā as the unnameable belly-mover, is unique to this Old Javanese Tattvajñāna text; Vrhaspatitattva enumerates similar Saiva tattvas and discusses Daityas and Rākṣasas but never strings them into this specific yoga-failure ladder, and no Sanskrit corpus text (Manu, Yājñavalkya, the smṛtis, Vīramitrodaya) lists Bhūta Dĕṅĕn or this ordered devolution.

prompt: Which work in the corpus assigns three classes of named blades to three patron divinities by social function — declaring that the Rākṣasa is the patron divinity of the king's life-taking weapons such as the curik, malalya, and śaṅkuh; that the Daitya is the patron divinity of the farmer's livelihood-producing implements such as the luke and pṛkul (or prĕkul); and that the Dānava is the patron divinity of the priest's ritual knives such as the pamiso (or pakisi), kartrī, and karayu — and immediately frames this within a blacksmith simile in which raw iron, tongs, hammer, anvil, and file all answer to the smith's will, the blade alone striking good and evil while the tools do not? Quote the three blade-divinity assignments verbatim and identify the work and chapter.
rationale: The exact tripartite king/farmer/priest blade typology with the named indigenous blade-types (curik, malalya, śaṅkuh; luke, pṛkul; pamiso/pakisi, kartrī, karayu) and their pairing with Rākṣasa/Daitya/Dānava as patron divinities is unique to this Old Javanese text; Vrhaspatitattva uses 'blacksmith' only as an example of a worldly occupation and does not contain this typology, and no Sanskrit smṛti, dharmaśāstra digest, or other corpus work pairs these specific Old Javanese weapon-names with these classes of demonic patrons.
